emotions/kiss.md.title: kiss
emotions/kiss.md.body:
# kiss

**Part of Speech:** noun / verb
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** 키스 — Touch with the lips as a sign of love, greeting, or respect.

## Examples (Noun)

- "First kiss" — 첫 키스
- "Goodnight kiss" — 작별 키스
- "Peck on the cheek" — 가벼운 키스

## Examples (Verb)

- "Can I kiss you?" — 명시적 동의 요청 (중요)
- "They kissed at the airport." — 장면 묘사
- "Kiss me." — 직접적 요청

## Related Terms

- [[hold-your-hand]] — earlier progression
- [[love]] — strong affection
- [[be-my-girlfriend]] — commitment

## Cultural Notes

**Consent is essential**: Modern dating requires explicit verbal
permission before physical affection. **"Can I kiss you?"** is now
the expected, respectful approach — not awkward, but considerate.

**Public displays**: Common in US/Europe, varies by region/context.

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Affection progression
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Pre-Intermediate)

i-love-you.md.title: i-love-you
i-love-you.md.body:
# i-love-you

**Part of Speech:** expression (deep confession)
**Definition:** "사랑해" — Deep romantic confession. Most weighted phrase in English romance.

**Form:** I love you.

## Usage

**English romance의 정점**. 이전 단계: "I like you" → "I really
like you" → "I love you". 점진적 상승.

## Variations

- **I love you.** — 표준, 정중
- **I love ya.** — 캐주얼 (친구, 가족, 연인 모두)
- **I'm in love with you.** — 더 깊음 (감정적 깊이 강조)
- **Love you!** — 일상적 작별 인사 (영어권)

## Cultural Notes

**Timing matters**:
- US: typically 2-3 months in
- UK: often later
- Australia: relatively early

**Saying it too early** can be uncomfortable. **Receiving it
unexpectedly** can be a relationship-defining moment.

**Same-sex usage**: identical.

## Related Expressions

- [[really-like-you]] — lighter version
- [[be-my-girlfriend]] — formal commitment
- [[stay-friends]] — if not reciprocated

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Affection progression
- Notting Hill (1999) — iconic scene

really-like-you.md.body:
# really-like-you

**Part of Speech:** expression (confession, partial)
**Definition:** "난 정말 너를 좋아해" — Strong but not full confession.

**Form:** I really like you.

## Usage

호감 표현의 중간 단계. "like" 보다 강하지만 "love" 전 단계.

## Variations

- **I like you.** — 약한 호감
- **I really like you.** — 확고한 호감

this as the actual "I love you" precursor in serious relationships.

english-holidays-culture.md.title: English Holidays & Celebrations Culture
english-holidays-culture.md.body:
# English Holidays & Celebrations Culture

**Type:** holidays, festivals, seasonal traditions
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking countries share many holidays but celebrate them differently. **Christmas**, **Thanksgiving**, and **Easter** are pan-English-culture holidays, while each country has unique additions (Guy Fawkes in the UK, Thanksgiving in the US/Canada).

## Key Cultural Traits

### American Holidays

**Christmas (Dec 25):**
- Decorations go up November 1 in stores, but Americans decorate their homes after Thanksgiving (late November)
- Santa Claus is central — children write letters to Santa
- Key phrase: "Merry Christmas!" or "Happy holidays!" (the latter is inclusive)

**Thanksgiving (4th Thursday, November):**
- Family dinner with turkey, stuffing, cranberry sauce
- "Black Friday" (day after) is the biggest shopping day of the year
- Key phrase: "What are you thankful for?"

**Halloween (October 31):**
- Children trick-or-treat; adults dress up
- Key phrase: "Trick or treat!" + "Thank you!"

### British Holidays

**Bonfire Night (November 5):**
- "Remember, remember, the fifth of November" — Guy Fawkes Night
- Fireworks, bonfires, toffee apples

**Boxing Day (December 26):**
- Post-Christmas shopping day and sports events

### Australian Holidays

**Australia Day (January 26):**
- Controversial due to Indigenous perspectives ("Invasion Day")

**ANZAC Day (April 25):**
- Commemorates Australian and New Zealand soldiers
- Dawn vigils, marches

## Holiday Greetings (Register Guide)

| Holiday | Formal | Casual |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Christmas | "Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year!" | "Merry Christmas!" |
| Thanksgiving | "Happy Thanksgiving!" | "Hope you're having a great Thanksgiving!" |
| New Year | "Happy New Year!" | "Happy New Year! See you next year!" |
| Easter | "Happy Easter!" | "Hope you have a good Easter!" |

## Sources

- [[holidays-and-celebrations]] — holiday vocabulary

english-travel-culture.md.title: English Travel Culture
english-travel-culture.md.body:
# English Travel Culture

**Type:** travel, cross-cultural awareness
**Region:** UK / US / Canada / Australia / general English-speaking world

## Overview

English-speaking cultures (especially British and American) approach travel and tourism with a distinctive blend of **politeness conventions**, **informality in public spaces**, and a strong **personal-space awareness**. Unlike East Asian cultures where group harmony is prioritized, Western travel culture emphasizes individual autonomy and direct communication.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Politeness Formula

British and American English speakers almost always preface requests with **"Excuse me"** or **"Sorry"** even when they haven't done anything wrong:

> "Excuse me, where is the station?"
> "Sorry, could you help me?"

This is not genuine apology — it's a **softener** that signals respect for the listener's attention.

### Personal Space (Proxemics)

Western cultures typically maintain **1.2–2 meters** of personal space in public. Standing closer can feel intrusive. On public transport, sitting next to an empty seat is preferred.

### Tipping Culture

| Country | Tip at Restaurant | Tip at Taxi | Notes |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **US** | 15–20% | 15–20% | Expected, not optional |
| **UK** | 10% optional | Round up | Not mandatory |
| **Australia** | Not expected | Round up | Service included |
| **Canada** | 15% | 10–15% | Similar to US |

> **Japan tip = 0%** — Tipping is considered rude. English learners traveling to Japan must *unlearn* the tipping reflex.

### Public Transport Etiquette

- **UK (London Tube):** Stand on the right of escalators, walk on the left
- **US (subway):** Let passengers exit before boarding
- **Australia:** Queue orderly; pushing is socially condemned
- **Silence norm:** Phone calls on trains/buses are discouraged

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | Formal | Casual |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Asking for directions | "Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to...?" | "Hey, where's...?" |
| At a hotel | "I'd like to check in, please." | "Hi, I have a reservation." |
| Ordering food | "Could I please have the menu?" | "Can I see the menu?" |

## Cross-Cultural Notes

### "I" vs. "We" in Travel Contexts

American travelers often use **"we"** to soften requests:
> "Are we open?" (meaning: "Is the shop open yet?")
> "Can we get a table for two?" (at a restaurant)

British speakers are more likely to use **indirect phrasing**:
> "I wonder if you could help me..."
> "Would you happen to know where..."

## Related Expressions

- [[where-is]] — asking for locations
- [[how-much]] — asking for prices

## Sources

- [[first-travel-japan]] — cross-cultural travel scenario
- [[travel-basics]] — practical vocabulary

english-sports-culture.md.title: English Sports & Hobbies Culture
english-sports-culture.md.body:
# English Sports & Hobbies Culture

**Type:** sports culture, leisure activities, fandom
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

Sports are a **social identity marker** in English-speaking countries. Supporting a team is often a **family tradition** passed down through generations. "Who do you support?" (= Which team do you support?) is a common ice-breaker.

## Key Cultural Traits

### British Football (Soccer)

Football is the UK's national sport. Key features:

- **Club loyalty** is lifelong and hereditary: "I've supported Manchester United since I was five."
- **Casual match-goers** vs. **ultras** (die-hard fans in dedicated sections)
- **"It's coming home"** — phrase about England winning a major tournament (sarcastic optimism)
- Key phrases:
  > "What was the score?" (= What was the result?)
  > "We're playing United this weekend."

### American Sports

| Sport | Key League | Key Phrase |
|-------|-----------|------------|
| American Football | NFL | "Super Bowl" (Feb, biggest TV event) |
| Baseball | MLB | "Play ball!" / "Home run!" |
| Basketball | NBA | "Slam dunk!" |
| Ice Hockey | NHL | "Hat trick!" |

### "Fantasy" and Sports Talk

Americans love **fantasy sports** and statistics:
> "My fantasy team's killing it this season."
> "His stats are off the charts."

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | Phrase |
|-----------|--------|
| Inviting to play | "Do you want to play basketball?" |
| Score update | "We won 3–1!" |
| Watching together | "Want to catch the game?" (= watch together) |
| Disappointment | "We lost. It was embarrassing." |

## Sources

- [[sports-and-hobbies]] — sports vocabulary

english-technology-culture.md.title: English Technology & Digital Culture
english-technology-culture.md.body:
# English Technology & Digital Culture

**Type:** technology, digital etiquette, internet culture
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking countries, especially the US, are **digital-first societies**. Technology is deeply embedded in daily life, and digital etiquette ("netiquette") has developed its own norms. Understanding these cultural nuances helps English learners navigate online interactions authentically.

## Key Cultural Traits

### "I'm Offline" Culture

Americans and British use technology to signal **availability** and **social boundaries**:

> "I'm off the grid this weekend." (= I won't be reachable)
> "My phone died." (= My battery ran out)
> "I haven't been on social media for a week."

Being constantly connected is expected, but deliberately disconnecting is also socially acceptable.

### Email vs. Text

| Context | Medium | Tone |
|---------|--------|------|
| Work (formal) | Email | Professional, detailed |
| Work (informal) | Slack/Teams | Casual, emoji OK |
| Friends/family | Text/WhatsApp | Very casual |
| Dating | Text (initially) | Casual, flirty |
| Emergency | Phone call | Direct |

**American email:** Ends with "Best," or "Thanks," — never "Love,"
**British email:** May end with "Kind regards," or "Yours sincerely,"

### Phone Call Norms

Americans are **comfortable with phone calls**. British may find unsolicited calls intrusive. Key phrases:

> "Can I call you back?" (= Can I return your call?)
> "You have the wrong number."
> "The line is bad." (= The connection is poor)

### Social Media Register

| Platform | Register | Audience |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Twitter/X | Punchy, opinionated | Public |
| LinkedIn | Professional | Work |
| Instagram | Visual, captions | Friends/followers |
| TikTok | Casual, comedic | Broad |
| Facebook | Family-friendly | Mixed |

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | Phrase |
|-----------|--------|
| Asking for wifi | "What's the wifi password?" |
| Tech support | "My wifi isn't working." |
| Downloading | "I need to download an app." |
| Password reset | "I forgot my password." |

## Sources

- [[technology-and-internet]] — tech vocabulary

# English Health & Body Culture

**Type:** health, wellness, body image, medical culture
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking cultures (especially American) have a complex relationship with **health and wellness**. There's a strong culture of **"health talk"**, body positivity mixed with body consciousness, and a significant **medical-industrial complex** that shapes how health is discussed and accessed.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Health as Conversation

Americans and British discuss health openly:

> "How are you feeling?" (genuine concern or greeting)
> "I'm under the weather." (= slightly ill)
> "I'm feeling better, thanks."

Note: "How are you?" is often rhetorical — a simple "Fine, thanks!" is sufficient.

### Medical System

| Country | System | Key phrase |
|---------|--------|------------|
| **US** | Insurance-based (expensive) | "I don't have insurance." |
| **UK** | NHS (free at point of use) | "I'll book a doctor's appointment." |
| **Canada** | Medicare (provincial) | "I need to see a doctor." |
| **Australia** | Medicare + private | "Bulk billing" |

**American context:** Medical costs are a major source of anxiety. Insurance terms are complex:
> "I have a $50 copay." (= You pay $50 per visit)
> "It's out of network." (= Not covered by your insurance)

### Body Language & Health Talk

- **Weight:** Generally avoided as a direct topic unless the person brings it up. "You look great!" is a safe compliment.
- **Exercise:** Very common topic. "Do you work out?" is not intrusive.
- **Diet:** "I'm trying to eat healthy" is a common conversation opener.

## Common Expressions

| Situation | Phrase |
|-----------|--------|
| Seeing a doctor | "I need to make an appointment." |
| At the pharmacy | "Do I need a prescription for this?" |
| Feeling ill | "I have a headache / stomachache / sore throat." |
| Allergies | "I'm allergic to peanuts." |
| Emergencies | "Call an ambulance!" |

## Sources

- [[health-and-body]] — health vocabulary

english-business-culture.md.body:
# English Business & Work Culture

**Type:** Culture Note, Business, Workplace, Etiquette
**Region:** United States / United Kingdom / International Business

## Overview

English-speaking workplace culture emphasizes **direct communication**, **individual accountability**, and **results-oriented** thinking. Unlike Japanese or Korean workplaces, hierarchies are flatter and open disagreement is expected.

## Overview (Korean)

영어권 직장 문화는 **직접적인 소통**, **개인적 책임**, **성과 지향**을 중시. 일본이나 한국의 직장文化和 달리 계층이 평평하고 열린 의견 불일치가 기대됨.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Communication Style

| Aspect | US/UK Business Norm |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Directness** | Say what you mean, be specific |
| **Titles** | First names common in US; Mr./Ms. in formal UK |
| **Meetings** | Start on time, agenda-driven |
| **Feedback** | Constructive criticism is normal and expected |
| **Email tone** | Semi-formal; "Hi" not "Dear" in US |

### Meeting Culture

> "Let's take this offline" = Let's discuss this privately later.
> "Per my last email" = I already explained this.
> "Circle back" = We'll revisit this topic.

**Meeting Phases:**
1. **Agenda** — What's discussed
2. **Action Items** — Who does what by when
3. **Follow-up** — Accountability check

### Business Email Register

| Situation | US | UK |
|-----------|----|----|
| Opening | "Hi [Name]," | "Dear [Name]," |
| Request | "Could you please..." | "I wonder if you might..." |
| Closing | "Thanks," / "Best," | "Kind regards," / "Yours sincerely," |
| Very formal | "Dear Sir/Madam," | "Dear Sir/Madam," |

## Sources

- [[work-and-career]] — workplace vocabulary

english-daily-life-culture.md.title: English Daily Life Culture
english-daily-life-culture.md.body:
# English Daily Life Culture

**Type:** Culture Note, Daily Life, Routines, Social
**Region:** United States / United Kingdom / Australia / Canada

## Overview

English-speaking daily life centers on **individual schedules** and **punctuality**. Social interactions are friendly but relatively informal. Small talk about weather, weekend plans, and sports fills silence.

## Overview (Korean)

영어권 일상은 **개인의 일정**과 **시간 엄수**가 중심. 사회적 교류는 친절하지만 비교적 캐주얼. 날씨, 주말 계획, 스포츠에 대한 가벼운 대화로 침묵을 메움.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Greetings & Small Talk

English speakers use **small talk** to build rapport — not inauthentic, but social lubricant:

- "Lovely weather, isn't it?"
- "How was your weekend?"
- "Did you catch the game?"

> **Korean/English difference:** "How are you?" = greeting, NOT a real question. Answer briefly and move on.

### Daily Routines

| Time | Activity | English Phrase |
|------|----------|----------------|
| Morning | Wake up, coffee, commute | "I commute by subway/bus" |
| Lunch | Quick meal, often at desk | "Working lunch" |
| Evening | Gym, cooking, TV | "Are you doing anything tonight?" |
| Weekend | Grocery shopping, chores | "Running errands" |

### Punctuality

| Country | Norm |
|---------|------|
| **US** | 5 min late = late |
| **UK** | 5 min late = slightly late |
| **Australia** | Very punctual |
| **Social events** | 5-15 min late is acceptable (dinner parties) |

## Sources

- [[daily-life-basics]] — everyday vocabulary

english-social-culture.md.title: English Social Culture
english-social-culture.md.body:
# English Social Culture

**Type:** social, communication, relationships, community
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking cultures have distinct social norms around **greetings**, **personal space**, **small talk**, and **community involvement**. Understanding these cultural patterns helps with natural communication.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Greetings & Introductions

Americans and British value **casual friendliness**:

> "Nice to meet you!"
> "How do you do?" (formal, British)
> "Hey, how's it going?" (casual, American)

### Small Talk

English speakers use **small talk** extensively:

- Weather
- Sports
- Weekend plans
- "How was your weekend?"
- "Did you catch the game?"

### Personal Space

| Culture | Distance | Touch |
|---------|----------|-------|
| **American** | Arm's length | Minimal |
| **British** | Closer than American | Very minimal |
| **Australian** | Similar to British | Slightly more open |

### Community

> "How can I help?" — community involvement is valued
> "Thank you so much!" — gratitude is expressed frequently

## Communication Style

### Direct vs Indirect

- **Americans**: Generally direct
- **British**: Often indirect, using understatement
- "Not bad" = quite good
- "We should do lunch sometime" = may not happen

### Formality

| Situation | US Style | UK Style |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Workplace | First names common | Titles longer lasting |
| Service | "How can I help you?" | "What can I do for you?" |
| Thanks | "Thanks a lot!" | "Cheers!" (informal) |

## Related Pages

- [[friend]] — friendship
- [[family]] — family relationships
- [[party]] — social gatherings

## Sources

- Cross-cultural communication research

english-shopping-culture.md.title: English Shopping & Money Culture
english-shopping-culture.md.body:
# English Shopping & Money Culture

**Type:** Culture Note, Shopping, Consumer, Prices
**Region:** United States / United Kingdom / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking countries have distinct shopping cultures: US has large malls and e-commerce; UK has high streets; Australia has Sunday markets. **Tipping**, **taxes not included**, and **return policies** are key differences from Korean/Japanese shopping.

## Overview (Korean)

영어권 쇼핑 문화: 미국은 대형 몰과 이커머스, 영국은 하이 스트리트, 호주는 선데이 마켓. **팁**, **별도 세금**, **반품 정책**이 한국/일본과 주요 차이.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Price Display

US: Prices shown **without tax** — you pay more at checkout.
UK/Australia: Prices include VAT/tax.

> US: "This shirt is $20"
> Checkout: $20 + tax = ~$22

### Tipping Culture

| Country | Restaurant | Taxi | Hotel |
|---------|-----------|------|-------|
| **US** | 15-20% mandatory-feeling | 15-20% | $1-2/bag |
| **UK** | 10% optional (service often included) | Round up | Optional |
| **Australia** | Not expected | Not expected | Not expected |

> "Tipping in the US isn't optional — servers earn $2.13/hr base."

### Shopping Venues

| Venue | US | UK |
|-------|----|----|
| Grocery | Walmart, Kroger, Whole Foods | Tesco, Sainsbury's, Waitrose |
| Department | Macy's, Nordstrom | Harrods, Selfridges |
| Fast fashion | Target, H&M | Primark, Topshop |
| Online | Amazon dominates | Amazon + local brands |

## Phrases

| Situation | Phrase | Context |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Asking price | "How much is this?" | |
| Discount | "Is this on sale?" | US: "sale" / UK: "sale" |
| Returns | "Can I return this?" | US: 30-90 days |
| Size | "Do you have this in a different size?" | |

## Sources

food/breakfast.md.title: breakfast
food/breakfast.md.body:
# breakfast

**Part of Speech:** noun
**Level:** A1

**Definition:** The first meal of the day, eaten in the morning (typically 6-10am).

**Etymology:** "Break" (to interrupt) + "fast" (going without food). Literally "the meal that breaks the fast of night." Coined ~15th century.

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈbrekfəst/
- Syllables: break-fast
- Stress: 1st syllable (BREAK-fast)

**Memory Tip:** "You **BREAK** your **FAST** (no food since dinner). Two long words combined = morning meal!"

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "I eat breakfast in the evening" → breakfast is a morning meal; use "dinner"/"supper" for evening
- ❌ "I have a breakfast" → NO article ("I have breakfast" / "I have SOME breakfast")
- ❌ "I am eating breakfast" → awkward; use "I'm having breakfast" or "I eat breakfast"
- ✅ "What's for breakfast?" (asking)
- ✅ "I had breakfast at 7." (specific time)
- ✅ "I skip breakfast." (don't eat it)
- Note: "Brunch" = breakfast + lunch (combined late morning meal, ~10am-1pm)

**Register:** neutral. Universally used.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 1000 English words)

## Examples

- "What's for breakfast?" — 아침 뭐 먹을까?
- "I had eggs for breakfast." — 아침에 계란 먹었어
- "We serve breakfast from 6 to 10." — 호텔/식당
- "Do you want to grab breakfast tomorrow?" — 친구/연인 (casual)
- "I never skip breakfast." — 일상 루틴

## Mini-Dialogue (Hotel)

```
A: Good morning! Breakfast is served from 6 to 10.   ← 좋은 아침! 조식은 6시부터 10시까지입니다.
B: Great, thank you. Is coffee included?   ← 잘됐네요, 감사해요. 커피 포함인가요?
A: Yes, all-you-can-eat buffet.   ← 네, 뷔페식입니다.
B: Perfect! I'll be down in 10 minutes.   ← 완벽해요! 10분 후 내려갈게요.
```

## Related Terms

- [[lunch]] — midday meal
- [[dinner]] / [[supper]] — evening meal
- [[brunch]] — late morning combo
- [[meal]] — general term
- [[restaurant]] — place to eat
- [[coffee]] / [[tea]] — common drinks
- [[hotel]] — place that serves breakfast
- [[morning]] — time of breakfast

## Cultural Notes

- **"Continental breakfast"** = light European-style (bread, pastry, coffee, juice) — hotel term.
- **"American breakfast"** = eggs, bacon, toast, hash browns — heavier.
- **Korean breakfast** is often rice, soup, banchan — different from Western.
- **Japanese breakfast** typically includes rice, miso soup, fish, egg — savory, not sweet.
- **British breakfast** ("fry-up") = eggs, beans, sausage, bacon, toast — large, traditional.
- **"Breakfast in bed"** — romantic gesture, often anniversary or special occasion.

## Sources

- [[travel-basics]] — first travel
- [[hotel]] — accommodation
- [[restaurant]] — dining out
- [[first-travel-japan]]

english-food-culture.md.title: English Food & Dining Culture
english-food-culture.md.body:
# English Food & Dining Culture

**Type:** food culture, dining etiquette, cuisine
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia

## Overview

English-speaking cultures (especially American) have a distinctive relationship with food: **portion sizes are large**, **dining is social**, and **"food talk"** is a major conversation topic. Unlike East Asian cultures where cuisine is deeply ritualized, Western dining emphasizes **casual informality** combined with **polite restaurant etiquette**.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Portion Sizes

American portions are notoriously large. "Doggy bags" (leftovers to take home) are completely normal:

> "Can I get a box for this?" (= Can I have a takeout container?)

This is not considered cheap — it's practical and expected.

### Restaurant Culture

**Waiter culture:** In the US, waiters are **service professionals** who expect to be tipped (15–20%). Asking "How's it going?" is a genuine prompt — a chance to start a conversation.

**Seating:** In casual restaurants, you may be seated at a shared table ("communal seating"). In upscale restaurants, the table is your private space.

**Ordering:** Americans order directly:
> "I'll have the steak, medium rare, with the mashed potatoes."

British speakers are more tentative:
> "Could I possibly have the...?"

### Breakfast Culture

**American:** Large — eggs, bacon/sausage, pancakes, toast, hash browns, coffee
**British:** Smaller — cereal or toast and tea ("Full English" is a weekend treat)
**Australian:** Vegemite on toast (iconic), avocado on toast (modern classic)

### Food as Small Talk

Food is one of the **safest conversation topics** in English-speaking cultures:

> "How was your meal?"
> "What are you having?"
> "Have you tried the dessert?"

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | American | British |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Asking for the bill | "Can I get the check?" | "Can I have the bill please?" |
| Complimenting food | "This is amazing!" | "This is lovely, thank you." |
| Dietary restriction | "I'm vegetarian." | "I don't eat meat." |

## Related Expressions

- [[how-much]] — asking for prices
- [[where-is]] — asking for restaurant locations

## Sources

- [[food-and-dining]] — restaurant vocabulary

emotions/friend.md.title: friend
emotions/friend.md.body:
# friend

**Part of Speech:** noun
**Level:** A1

**Definition:** A person you like and trust, not family or romantic partner.

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /frend/
- Syllables: friend (1 syllable)

**Memory Tip:** A "friend" ends a fight — that's why they're called friend. Or: friend = "fr" + "iend" — the best person to "end" your week with!

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "He is my friend John" → "He is my friend, John" (appositive, comma)
- ❌ "a friends" → "a friend" (singular) / "friends" (plural)
- ❌ "friend with me" → "friend of mine" / "my friend"
- ✅ "We're friends" (state) vs "We're making friends" (action)
- Note: In Spanish "amigo/amiga", in Korean "친구 (chingu)", in Japanese "友達 (tomodachi)"

**Register:** universal — appropriate for all contexts.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 200 English words)

## Examples

- "She's my best friend." — 가장 친한 친구
- "We're going out with friends tonight." — 오늘 밤 친구들이랑 놀러 가요
- "Make new friends!" — 새 친구 사귀어!
- "Old friends are the best." — 오랜 친구가 최고
- "Are you two friends?" — 너 둘 친구야?

## Mini-Dialogue

```
A: How did you two meet?   ← 어떻게 만났어?
B: We were friends in college.   ← 대학 때 친구였어.
A: That's nice. Still close?   ← 좋네. 아직 친해?
B: Yeah, best friends for 10 years!   ← 응, 10년 넘게 절친이야!
```

## Related Terms

- [[boyfriend]] — 남자친구
- [[girlfriend]] — 여자친구
- [[family]] — 가족
- [[love]] — 사랑
- [[hello]] — 인사

## Cultural Notes

- **Best friend (BFF)**: Close friend, like family. Western cultures emphasize individualism but value deep friendships.
- **Korean 12-year cycle**: Korea has special friendship anniversaries (100일, 200일, etc.).
- **Friendzone**: Western term — when one wants romance but the other sees them as friend only.

## Sources

- [[daily-life-basics]] — 일상 어휘
- [[dating-romance]] — 친구 vs 연인 구분
- English Vocabulary in Use (Pre-Intermediate)

emotions/beautiful.md.title: beautiful
emotions/beautiful.md.body:
# beautiful

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Pleasing the senses or mind aesthetically. Used for people, places, things, ideas, actions.

**Etymology:** From Old French "beau" (handsome) + "-ful" suffix. "Beau" itself comes from Latin "bellus" (pretty, charming).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈbjuːtɪfəl/
- Syllables: beau-ti-ful
- Stress: 1st syllable (BEAU-ti-ful)

**Memory Tip:** "**BEAUtiFUL**" — "Beau" = French word for handsome. French men are stereotypically handsome, so the word carries elegance. When you see a BEAU, the reaction is "ti-ful" (too beautiful)!

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "You're a beautiful" → missing noun ("You're a beautiful person")
- ❌ "Beautifuller" / "More beautiful" — both OK, but "more beautiful" is more natural
- ❌ "Beautiful girl" to a stranger → can feel like catcalling in many Western contexts
- ✅ "That's a beautiful painting" (art/object — safe)
- ✅ "You look beautiful tonight" (event context — appropriate)
- Note: For men, use [[handsome]]. "Beautiful boy" sounds effeminate or ironic.

**Register:** semi-formal. Universally appropriate but considered slightly elevated/cordial.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 English words)

**Visual:** ```
  B·EAU  →  T I  →  F·UL
  (French "beau")  (suffix)
```

## Examples

- "You look beautiful tonight." — 칭찬, formal context (date, event)
- "What a beautiful day." — 일상, weather/scenes
- "Beautiful work on the project!" — 성과 칭찬, business
- "That's a beautiful name." — 개인적 호감 표현
- "Beautiful mind, beautiful soul." — 추상적, 시적

## Mini-Dialogue

```
A: Wow, this sunset is beautiful!   ← 와, 이 노을 정말 아름답다!
B: It really is. The colors are amazing.   ← 정말이야. 색깔이 놀랍다.
A: Want to take a photo together?   ← 같이 사진 찍을래?
B: I'd love that.   ← 좋지.
```

## Related Terms

- [[handsome]] — used for men
- [[cute]] — younger, playful vibe, less formal
- [[pretty]] — slightly less formal than beautiful; can describe people/art
- [[gorgeous]] — stronger compliment; "stunning"
- [[lovely]] — softer, more British; less intense
- [[attractive]] — neutral, can be physical/abstract

## Cultural Notes

- **Eye contact + smile** while saying "you look beautiful" is essential — alone it can feel creepy.
- In dating apps: "beautiful" is overused. "Gorgeous" or "stunning" stands out more.
- For compliments on appearance, **specify what** (dress, hair, eyes) — more sincere than generic.
- **Avoid** to strangers — in many cultures it's interpreted as harassment.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (appearance)
- Oxford Word Skills (Intermediate)
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Upper-Int)

emotions/handsome.md.title: handsome
emotions/handsome.md.body:
# handsome

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** (Of a man) good-looking, attractive. Can also apply to objects (a handsome building) or amounts (a handsome sum).

**Etymology:** From "hand" + Old English "sum" (easy, ready to hand). Originally meant "easy to handle, near at hand." Sense of "good-looking" developed ~16th century.

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈhænsəm/
- Syllables: hand-some
- Stress: 1st syllable (HAND-some)
- Note: "ds" is silent in pronunciation

**Memory Tip:** A **HAND-SOME** man is good enough to **handle** (a room, a date, a job interview). Confident, capable, attractive.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "She's handsome" → traditional rule: women = [[beautiful]]/[[pretty]], men = handsome
- ❌ "Handsomely" (as adjective) → "handsomely" is the adverb ("He was handsomely dressed")
- ❌ "A handsome" alone → needs a noun ("a handsome man" / "a handsome face")
- ✅ "He's really handsome." (describing a man)
- ✅ "What a handsome couple." (couple = mixed, both look good)
- ✅ "A handsome profit." (large, generous — different meaning!)
- Note: Modern usage is **more flexible** — "handsome woman" is becoming acceptable, especially for non-binary or strong-featured. But "beautiful" is safer default.

**Register:** semi-formal. Standard compliment.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐

## Examples

- "He's really handsome." — 외모 칭찬
- "What a handsome couple." — 부부/커플 칭찬
- "Handsome and charming." — 종합 칭찬
- "A handsome salary." — 넉넉한 급여 (different meaning)
- "A handsome building." — 웅장한 건물 (object)

## Mini-Dialogue (Complimenting)

```
A: That's a nice photo of your boyfriend.   ← 남자친구 사진 잘 나왔네.
B: Thanks! He's pretty handsome, right?   ← 고마워! 꽤 잘생기지, 맞아?
A: Definitely. And he looks kind too.   ← 확실히. 그리고 친절해 보여.
B: He really is. The total package.   ← 진짜 그래. 완벽해.
```

## Related Terms

- [[beautiful]] — usually for women / general beauty
- [[cute]] — playful, younger vibe; can apply to men
- [[pretty]] — women, less intense than beautiful
- [[gorgeous]] — stronger than handsome; "wow factor"
- [[attractive]] — neutral, can be physical/personality
- [[good-looking]] — casual alternative to handsome
- [[hunky]] — slang, very handsome (often muscular)
- [[cute]] / [[hot]] — younger generation, casual

## Cultural Notes

- Traditionally: **handsome = men**, **beautiful = women**, **pretty = women or gender-neutral**. Modern usage is more flexible.
- **Avoid overusing** "handsome" — feels generic. Specific compliments feel more genuine:
  - "You have a great smile."
  - "I love your sense of style."
  - "Your eyes are striking."
- **"Handsome" can describe objects** — a handsome car, a handsome profit. Old-fashioned but elegant.
- **Dating apps**: "handsome" is overused. Be specific to stand out.
- **Age factor**: "handsome" sounds slightly old-fashioned; younger people use "cute" or "hot" more.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (appearance)
- [[beautiful]] — comparison (women)
- Oxford Word Skills (Intermediate)

emotions/cute.md.title: cute
emotions/cute.md.body:

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

## Related Terms

Suitable for early stages of dating. Less formal than "beautiful" —

emotions/kind.md.title: kind
emotions/kind.md.body:
# kind

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Having or showing a friendly, generous, considerate nature. Describes consistent behavior toward others.

**Etymology:** Old English "gecynde" (natural, native). Sense of "good-natured" developed ~13th century. Cognate with "kind" (noun, meaning type/category) — both from "kin" (family).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /kaɪnd/
- Syllables: kind (1 syllable)
- Note: Same pronunciation as "kind" (type) — context disambiguates
- "Kind to me" = adjective (good-natured)
- "What kind?" = noun (type)

**Memory Tip:** "**KIND**" person is a **KIN**dred soul (family-like). You treat them like family = kind. Or: "Kin-d" → family + goodness = kindness.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "You're kind" alone → can sound like a diagnosis ("You have a kind heart" — awkward)
- ❌ "Kind person" (always with article) → "You're a kind person"
- ❌ "She is very kindly" (as adjective) → "She is very kind" (kind is the adjective; kindly is the adverb)
- ✅ "That's so kind of you." (gratitude — most common use)
- ✅ "He's kind to everyone." (describing behavior)
- ✅ "Kind words" / "kind thoughts" (noun use — types of)
- Note: "Kind" describes **action**, not appearance. For appearance use [[handsome]], [[beautiful]].

**Register:** universally appropriate, sincere. Slightly elevated in writing.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 English words)

## Examples

- "You're really kind." — 성격 칭찬
- "That's so kind of you." — 감사 (very common response to help)
- "He's kind to everyone." — 일관된 친절
- "It was kind of strange." — 약간 (= "sort of" — different meaning!)
- "Kind regards" — 이메일/편지 맺음말

## Mini-Dialogue (Friend)

```
A: Thanks for helping me move today!   ← 오늘搬家 도와줘서 고마워!
B: No problem. That's what friends are for.   ← 천만에. 그게 친구하는 일이지.
A: You're really kind, you know that?   ← 너 진짜 친절하다, 알지?
B: Aw, you're making me blush.   ← 에이, 부끄럽잖아.
```

## Related Terms

- [[nice]] — weaker, more general; "nice" is overused
- [[good]] — broader (kind of person, good at math, good food)
- [[sweet]] — warmer, more affectionate; "That's sweet of you"
- [[generous]] — emphasizes giving, sharing
- [[considerate]] — thinks of others' needs
- [[gentle]] — soft-spoken, mild
- [[caring]] — actively takes care of others

## Cultural Notes

- "Kind" is consistently rated one of the **most attractive traits** in English-speaking dating surveys (above "smart" or "funny" for long-term relationships).
- A **sincere compliment** — describes consistent behavior, not just appearance.
- **"Kind of"** (adjective + of) = "sort of" (somewhat). Different meaning. "I'm kind of tired" = "I'm somewhat tired."
- **"Kind regards"** is a professional sign-off — common in business emails.
- "Be kind" has become a cultural movement (mental health awareness, social media).
- **Polite culture**: "That's so kind" is a common response to ANY small favor. Reinforces social bonds.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (personality)
- [[nice]] — comparison
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Pre-Intermediate)

# hold-your-hand

**Part of Speech:** expression (intimacy request)
**Definition:** "손 잡아도 돼?" — Consent request for first physical affection.

**Form:** Can I hold your hand?

## Usage

**신체 친밀의 첫 단계** + **명시적 동의** 요청. 현대 영어권의 매너.

## Cultural Notes

**Consent is the standard**: Modern dating ethics in English-speaking
countries require **explicit verbal permission** before physical
intimacy. "Can I hold your hand?" is **expected, not awkward**.

**Not asking** = potential red flag.

**Earlier stage**: "Want to walk together?" (just being close).
Then: holding hands.
Then: arm around waist.
Then: kiss (also with consent).

english-dating-culture.md.title: English Dating Culture
english-dating-culture.md.body:
# English Dating Culture

**Type:** romance, social-ritual
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia (English-speaking countries)

## Overview

English-speaking countries (especially US) have **explicit, individualist** dating culture. Romantic intent is usually stated clearly, consent is verbal, and personal boundaries are respected. Modern dating emphasizes direct communication, gender equality (in principle), and emotional honesty.

## Setting

A first date in the US/UK: **coffee shop, restaurant, bar, or walk in a park**. Evening (7-9pm typical). Casual to smart-casual dress. Two people meeting with romantic intent, getting to know each other.

## Roles

- **Person who invites (inviter)** — takes initiative, suggests time/place
- **Invitee** — can accept, suggest alternatives, or decline politely
- Both pay attention to comfort, consent, and clear communication

## Scenario: A Typical First Date

1. **Planning** — Inviter suggests a specific time/place ("Want to grab coffee Saturday at 3?")
2. **Acceptance** — Invitee confirms or reschedules
3. **Arrival** — Both arrive on time (5 min early is normal); greet with smile + light hug or wave
4. **Conversation** — Mix of light (hobbies, work) and deeper (values, life goals) topics
5. **Physical contact** — Wait for clear signals; ask before initiating hand-holding or kissing
6. **Bill** — Often offered by inviter, but splitting is normal; "Let's split?" is OK
7. **End** — Confirm interest: "I had a great time, want to do this again?"
8. **Follow-up text** — Within 24 hours: "Thanks for tonight, I really enjoyed it."

## Cultural Insight

### "Hanging Out" vs "Dating"

In US English, "date" implies **explicit romantic intent**. "Hang out" is ambiguous — could be friends or romantic. Modern dating advice encourages **clarifying intent early** to avoid misunderstandings.

**"Are we hanging out or dating?"** is a real conversation that happens. Best to be clear from the start.

### Splitting the Bill

**Generation gap**:
- Older generations: man traditionally pays
- Younger (Z, Millennials): 50/50 split is normal
- Most common: person who invited pays, but offering to split is polite

**Tactful**: Don't insist either way. "Let's split it?" or "I've got this one" are both fine.

### Consent Culture

Modern English-speaking countries (especially US/Canada/UK) have embraced **explicit verbal consent**:
- "Can I hold your hand?"
- "Can I kiss you?"
- "Is this okay?"

Not asking is now considered a **red flag**. Asking is **mature**, not awkward.

### "I Love You" Timing

- **US**: typically 2-3 months into a relationship
- **UK**: often later (3-6 months)
- **Australia**: relatively early (1-2 months)

Earlier expression: "I really like you" (more universally safe).

### Personal Space

- **Friends/acquaintances**: ~45cm (18in)
- **Romantic partners**: closer, touching common
- **Strangers**: arm's length+

**Eye contact**: 60-70% during conversation = interested.

### Online / Text Dating

- **Apps**: Tinder, Bumble, Hinge dominant in US/UK
- **First contact**: text/messaging common
- **Phone call**: more sincere, less common
- **Asking out**: text is fine; in-person or call for serious intent

## Useful Phrases

- [[i-like-you]] — early affection
- [[i-love-you]] — deep feeling
- [[beautiful]] — appearance compliment
- [[handsome]] — male appearance compliment
- [[kind]] — personality compliment
- [[can-i-kiss-you]] — consent request
- [[would-you-like-to]] — invitation
- [[i-had-a-great-time]] — end-of-date
- [[see-you-again]] — follow-up

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **"Wanna date?"** in first message — too direct
- ❌ **Bombarding texts** — turn-off
- ❌ **"You up?"** late-night texts — usually means casual sex
- ❌ **Love bombing** — excessive flattery early
- ❌ **Ghosting** — disappearing without explanation (avoidable but common)
- ❌ **"You're so beautiful"** to a stranger on the street — catcalling, harassment
- ❌ **Asking weight, age, salary** on first date — too personal
- ❌ **Talking only about yourself** — reciprocal conversation expected

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Eye contact 60-70% | Interested, engaged |
| Mirroring (leaning in, gestures) | Comfortable, in sync |
| Looking at phone during date | Disinterested, rude |
| Fidgeting, looking around | Nervous or bored |
| Touching face/hair (other person) | Often flirting, but not always |
| Hand on table palm up | Inviting touch |
| Turning body toward you | Engaged |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[japanese-dating-culture]], [[spanish-dating-culture]], [[korean-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[i-love-you]], [[beautiful]], [[handsome]]

## Holiday / Special Days

- **Valentine's Day** (Feb 14) — big romantic holiday
- **Anniversaries** — celebrate relationship start
- **No formal "100 days" celebration** (unlike Korean culture)

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — main source
- BBC Learning English — Relationships
- The Atlantic — Modern dating essays
- OKCupid / Hinge dating blog posts
- Consent culture resources (Planned Parenthood, etc.)

emotions/love.md.title: love
emotions/love.md.body:
# love

**Part of Speech:** verb / noun
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** An intense feeling of deep affection. As verb: to feel this way toward someone/something. As noun: the feeling itself.

**Etymology:** Old English "lufu" (love, affection). Cognate with Old High German "luba" (love). Indo-European root *leubh- (to love, desire).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /lʌv/ (as noun AND verb — same pronunciation!)
- Syllables: love (1 syllable)
- Note: The "e" is silent

**Memory Tip:** "**L**ong-term **O**verwhelming **V**ivid **E**motion" — L.O.V.E. The four stages of intense feeling: lasting, overwhelming, vivid, eternal.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "I love to you" → "I love you"
- ❌ "Love you!" to a stranger → feels like a prank
- ❌ "I'm loving it" (McDonald's slogan) — grammatically OK in present continuous, but use "I love it" for serious contexts
- ✅ "I love you" — direct, sincere, romantic
- ✅ "I'd love to" — accepting an invitation (NOT romantic)
- Note: "Love" appears in **escalation**: like → really like → love. Don't skip steps with people.

**Register:** "I love you" = serious/deep. "Love ya!" = casual. "I'd love to" = neutral invitation response.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 200 English words)

## Examples (Verb)

- "I love you." — 진지한 사랑 고백
- "I love this song." — 강한 호감 (취향)
- "Love ya!" — 캐주얼 (친구, 가족)
- "I'd love to come." — 초대 수락
- "She loves chocolate." — 취향

## Examples (Noun)

- "Love is patient, love is kind." — 격언 (성경, 영화 인용)
- "Falling in love" — 사랑에 빠지다
- "True love" — 진짜 사랑
- "Love at first sight" — 첫눈에 반함
- "Make love, not war" — 유명 슬로건 (1960s)

## Mini-Dialogue (Romantic)

```
A: I've been thinking about you a lot.   ← 당신에 대해 많이 생각했어요.
B: Me too. I feel the same way.   ← 나도. 나도 같은 마음이야.
A: I think I'm falling in love with you.   ← 나 당신을 사랑에 빠진 것 같아.
B: I love you too.   ← 나도 당신을 사랑해.
A: I love you.   ← 사랑해.
```

## Related Terms

- [[like]] — weaker feeling (escalation)
- [[miss]] — feeling of absence
- [[adore]] — stronger than love (poetic)
- [[cherish]] — protective love
- [[heart]] — symbol of love
- [[kiss]] — physical expression
- [[boyfriend]] / [[girlfriend]] — person you love

## Cultural Notes

- **Timing matters**: In US, "I love you" typically 2-3 months into dating. In UK, often later. In Korea/Japan, sometimes years. Earlier safer expression: "I really like you a lot" (US) or "좋아해" (KR).
- **"I love you" vs "Love ya"**: same words, VERY different weight. "Love ya" = friendly, casual (friends, family). "I love you" = serious (romantic).
- **Same-sex usage**: same conventions; no separate phrase needed.
- **"Fall in love"** is romantic; "feel love" is general.
- **"Make love"** = sexual; "have sex" = more direct. Different registers.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Affection progression, timing norms
- [[i-love-you]] — the phrase itself
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Intermediate)

japanese-travel-culture.md.title: 日本旅行文化 (Japanese Travel Culture)
japanese-travel-culture.md.body:
# 日本旅行文化 (Japanese Travel Culture)

**Type:** 旅行, 文化比較, 実地学習
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の旅行文化は**準備性と礼儀**が基盤. 外国人旅行者が最も戸惑うのは**チップ文化がない**こと、**公共交通機関の時刻厳守**、そして**迷惑をかけない**という集団意識.

## Overview (English)

Japanese travel culture is built on **preparation** and **consideration for others**. Foreign travelers are most often confused by: no tipping, punctual public transport, and the strong group-consciousness norm of "don't cause trouble for others."

## 日本の旅行の基本原則

### 1. 時刻厳守 (じこくげんしゅ)

電車・バス・新幹線は**分単位**で正確. 「5分遅刻」は遅刻.

> "新幹線は正確です。何分든지遅れることは珍しいです。"

### 2. チップなし

レストラン・タクシー・ホテルのサービスにチップを渡さない. 渡すと**失礼** perceptionされる可能性がある.

> 欧米人：「サービスに満足しました。 tipをあげます。」
> 日本人：「ええと...？」（困惑）

### 3. 迷惑防止 (めいわくぼうし)

公共の場では：
- 電車での大声通話は避ける
- 優先席附近ではスマホのマナーモード
- 飲食は指定区域のみ

## 重要な語彙

| Situation | Japanese | Romaji | Meaning |
|---------|----------|--------|---------|
| 駅在哪里? | 駅はどこですか? | eki wa doko desu ka? | Where is the station? |
| 切符購入 | 切符を買います | kippu wo kaimasu | Buy a ticket |
| 席譲る | 席を譲ります | seki wo yuzurimasu | Offer a seat |

## 文化比較

| Aspect | 日本 | 英語圏 |
|--------|------|--------|
| 時刻 | 分単位の正確さ | 5-10分迟到は普通 |
| チップ | なし | 15-20% |
| 公共の場での通話 | 控える | 比較的自由 |
| 地図サービス | Google マップが一般的 | Waze, Google Maps |

## Sources

- [[first-travel-japan]] — 旅行シナリオ
- [[travel-basics-jp]] — 基本語彙

japanese-technology-culture.md.body:
# 日本のテクノロジー文化 (Japanese Technology Culture)

**Type:** テクノロジー, デジタルエチケット, SNS文化
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本人はテクノロジーに詳しい国民として知られています。しかし、**電子メール·ビジネス文化**は欧米と異なる面较多.

## 日本のテクノロジーの特徴

### デジタル機器への愛

日本人は最新ガジェットの Released を熱狂的に追跡. **秋葉原 (Akihabara)** は世界のテクノロジー愛好家の聖地.

> 「最新モデルのスマートフォンが発売されます。」
> "The newest smartphone model is being released."

### 電車内でのTechnology

日本の電車では：
- **優先席附近**ではケイタイのマナーモード必須
- 通話は控える —  사람들은 대부분スマホを見るか、静かにしている
- **車内Wifi**を提供する新幹線・飛行機が増加中

### ソーシャルメディア

| プラットフォーム | 日本での名称 | Notes |
|----------------|-------------|-------|
| Twitter/X | >X (旧Twitter) | 政治·アニメ文化が盛ん |
| LINE | ライン | ーメイン連絡ツール |
| Instagram | -instance | 画像共有が主流 |
| TikTok |  틱톡 | Z世代に人気 |

## ビジネスTechnology

日本のビジネスメールは**非常に形式的**:

> 件名: ご Jornment のご案内
> 本文: お世話になっております...
> 結語: 敬请 股 Shelf をご覧ください。

**注意:** 日本のビジネスメールは「了解しました」より「承知いたしました」が適切.

## Sources

- [[technology-and-internet-jp]] — テクノロジー語彙

japanese-health-culture.md.title: 日本健康·医療文化 (Japanese Health & Medical Culture)
japanese-health-culture.md.body:
# 日本健康·医療文化 (Japanese Health & Medical Culture)

**Type:** 健康, 医療システム, 身体観
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の医療制度は**国民皆保険制度**で、自己負担は3割. 药店( Drugstore )文化と町の，医院(Clinic) 文化が并存.

## Overview (English)

Japan has universal healthcare (you pay 30% of costs). The healthcare system combines **drugstore culture** (for minor ailments) with **clinic culture** (for serious issues).

## 日本の医疗制度

| 项目 | 日本 | 美国 |
|------|------|------|
| 医疗保险 | 国民皆保険 (3割負担) | 民间保险 (高額) |
| 診察 | 病院·医院 (先に予約) | Emergency Room (重的才行) |
| 药店 | ディズライ drugstore (カウンセ相談可) | Pharmacy (別) |
| 健康診断 | 每年必須 (企業に義務) | 任意 |

## 药店文化

日本のディズライ drugstoreは**水の惑星**:

- 日用品·化妆品·食品·药物が揃う
- カウンセが常駐している店舗が多い
- **第一類医薬品**はカウンセ相談后才販売

> 「頭が痛いです。 推荐は何ですか？」
> "I have a headache. What do you recommend?"

## 日本の身体の表念

日本人は**間接的に**健康を話題にする:

- 「お加減はどうですか？」(间接问候)
- 「最近ちょっと調子が悪くて...」(自己の非公開)
- 「お大事にしてください」(見舞い)

## Sources

- [[health-and-body-jp]] — 健康語彙

business/綺麗.md.title: 綺麗
business/綺麗.md.body:
# 綺麗

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (な-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Beautiful (people, things, scenery); clean; pure. Two main senses: (1) visual beauty, (2) cleanliness/order.

**読み方:** きれい (kirei)

**Etymology:** 綺 (beautiful brocade) + 麗 (elegant). Originally described ornate textiles; shifted to general beauty by Heian period (8-12c).

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: きれい (kirei)
- Pitch: 頭高 (L-H-H-H) — き↓れ↑い↑  / alternatively H-H-H-H
- For 奇麗: きれい (same reading, kanji variant)
- For 美: うつくしい (utsukushii) — formal/literary

**Memory Tip:** 綺麗 → KIREI → "**KI**nd **RE**flection **I**magined" — 3 syllables, the i-adj echo of "kire-i". Or: "**K**eep **I**t **R**eally **E**legant **I**n mind" = きれい (beautiful).

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「綺麗の人」 → 「綺麗な人」(na-adj needs な before noun)
- ❌ 「綺麗じゃないで」 → 「綺麗じゃないです」(polite form)
- ❌ 「綺麗」to a stranger → feels too forward; reserve for established relationship
- ❌ 「綺麗でした」 → 「綺麗でした」OK for past events, but 「綺麗ですね」 more natural
- ✅ 「綺麗ですね」(present compliment — natural)
- ✅ 「部屋が綺麗」(clean room — different sense)
- ✅ 「綺麗に書きます」(do something beautifully/cleanly — adverbial にも)
- Note: For men use かっこいい or ハンサム (handsome), not 綺麗.

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate within established relationships.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   綺 ← silk, brocade (8 strokes)
       ↓
   麗 ← elegant, beautiful (19 strokes, complex)
   ─────────
   綺麗 = ornate + elegant = beautiful
```

## Examples

- 「綺麗ですね」 — You're beautiful (compliment, neutral politeness)
- 「部屋が綺麗」 — The room is clean
- 「綺麗な花」 — Beautiful flower
- 「綺麗にしてください」 — Please make it clean / please clean it up
- 「お皿を綺麗にする」 — To clean the dishes (literally: make them beautiful)
- 「綺麗に書く」 — To write neatly/cleanly

## Mini-Dialogue (Compliment)

```
A: 今日、可愛いですね。   ← 오늘, 예쁘시네요.
B: ありがとう！ 綺麗って言ってくれて嬉しい。   ← 고마워! 綺麗라고 해줘서 기뻐.
A: 本当のことだよ。   ← 진심이야.
B: (笑) あなたもハンサムだよ。   ← (웃음) 당신도 잘생겼어요.
A: ありがとう。嬉しい。   ← 고마워. 기뻐.
```

## Variations

- 綺麗(きれい) — na-adj form, common
- 奇麗(きれい) — same reading, variant kanji (less common)
- 美(うつく)しい — formal/literary, "utsukushii"
- ビューティフル — katakana loanword, casual

## Related Terms

- [[可愛い]] — cute (younger, playful)
- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[かっこいい]] — cool/handsome (for men)
- [[ハンサム]] — handsome (loanword)
- [[美しい]] — beautiful (formal/literary)
- 美しい — kanji-only writing
- 清楚(せいそ) — pure, innocent (literary)

## Cultural Notes

- **주의**: 綺麗를 처음 만난 사람에게 즉시 사용하면 가벼워 보일 수 있음. **설정된 관계에서** 진심 어린 칭찬으로 사용.
- 여성에게 가장 흔한 외모 칭찬. 남성에게는 かっこいい 또는 ハンサム.
- **두 가지 의미** — beauty와 cleanliness. Context가 구분: 「部屋が綺麗」= clean room, 「花が綺麗」= beautiful flower.
- 「綺麗に〜する」패턴이 매우 생산적: 「綺麗にかける」(to write neatly), 「綺麗に食べる」(to eat everything cleanly), 「綺麗に忘れる」(to cleanly forget — emotional distance).
- **일본 특유의 미의식**: 綺麗 includes moral purity (綺麗好き = clean-freak, 綺麗な心 = pure heart).

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- TRY! N4 語彙
- みんなの日本語 中級

business/面白い.md.title: 面白い
business/面白い.md.body:
# 面白い

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (い-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Interesting; funny; amusing; enjoyable. Combines English "interesting" + "funny" + "enjoyable" in one word.

**読み方:** おもしろい (omoshiroi)

**Etymology:** 面(おも) = "face, surface" + 白(しろ)い = "white." Originally meant "having a clear/bright face" → vivid, distinctive. Shifted to "interesting" by Edo period (17-19c).

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: おもしろい (omoshiroi)
- Pitch: 平板 (L-L-L-H-H) — おもしろ↓い↑ (flat-low → い high)
- Type: い-adjective

**Memory Tip:** "**O**ne **MOSHI** **RO**und **I**nteresting" — when something hits 面白い, you want one more round. Or: "面白(おもしろう)い" = "face becomes white" = surprised face = interesting!

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「面白いです」 → 「面白いですね」(more natural with sentence-end particle)
- ❌ 「面白くない人」 → sounds harsh; prefer 「話が合わない」(we don't click)
- ❌ 「面白くありません」→ 「面白くないです」(i-adj, polite form)
- ❌ 「面白い人」 to mean "weird person" → 「面白い人」= positive (funny/interesting); use 「変な人」for weird
- ✅ 「面白い映画だった」(It was an interesting movie)
- ✅ 「彼、面白いね」(He's funny, isn't he)
- ✅ 「話が面白い」(The conversation is interesting)
- Note: 「面白い」can be **negative** if context is ironic: 「本当に面白いね」with sarcastic tone = "wow, how 'interesting'" (sarcastic).

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   面 = face, mask (9 strokes)
       ↓
   白 = white, plain (5 strokes)
   ─────────
   面白い = "face is white" = surprised, vivid, interesting
```

## Examples

- 「面白い人」 — Interesting/funny person
- 「面白い映画」 — Interesting movie
- 「話が面白い」 — Story/conversation is interesting
- 「面白いことない？」 — Nothing interesting happening? (asking)
- 「面白い話があるよ」 — I have an interesting story

## Mini-Dialogue (Date Idea)

```
A: 今週末、何する？   ← 이번 주말, 뭐 해?
B: 面白い映画があるんだ、見に行かない？   ← 재미있는 영화 있는데, 보러 갈래?
A: いいね！ 何の映画？   ← 좋아! 무슨 영화?
B: 韓国映画なんだけど... 試してみる？   ← 한국 영화인데... 시도해볼래?
A: もちろん！楽しみ。   ← 당연하지! 기대돼.
```

## Variations

- 面白(おもしろ)い — standard
- 楽(たの)しい — enjoyable (different nuance: more emotional/fun)
- 愉快(ゆかい) — cheerful, amusing (literary)
- 興味深い(きょうみぶかい) — intellectually interesting
- 笑(わら)える — laughable, funny (specific to humor)

## Related Terms

- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[カフェ]] — café (for date ideas)
- 楽しい — enjoyable
- 退屈(たいくつ) — boring (antonym)
- つまらない — boring (colloquial)
- 笑える — funny
- 興味深い — fascinating

## Cultural Notes

- **일본어에서 「面白い」= 재미있다/즐겁다/흥미로운** 모두 포함. 영어 "funny" + "interesting" + "enjoyable" 통합.
- 사람을 묘사할 때: **분위기 메이커, 대화가 잘 통하는 사람** 신호. 데이트에서 매우 중요한 매력.
- **"He/she is 面白い"** = they make you laugh, you want to spend more time with them, they have stories to tell.
- **음식에 사용**: 「この料理、面白い味だね」= "interesting taste" (not bad, but unusual).
- **드라마, 만화에서**: 거의 모든 "funny" 상황이 面白い로 표현됨.
- **비판적 사용**: 「本当に面白くないね」 = sarcastic, very cutting.

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- TRY! N4 文法
- 日本語中級J501

## Usage

처음 만났을 때 사용. "처음 뵙겠습니다"와 같은 격식. 그 후 "よろしくお願いします"로 이어짐.

**대화 흐름**:
1. はじめまして (처음 뵙겠습니다)

3. よろしくお願いします (잘 부탁드립니다)

**반응**: 상대도 같은 3단계로 답함.

**참고**: 같은 사람에게 두 번 사용하지 않음 (두 번째 만남부터는

好きです付き合ってください.md.body:
# 好きです付き合ってください

**Part of Speech:** 表現 (expression, formal confession)
**Definition:** "좋아합니다, 사귀어 주세요" — Standard formal confession.

**Form:** 好きです。付き合ってください。

## Usage

일본 연애 문화의 **고백의 정석**. 2-3개월 친밀해진 후, in-person.

## Variations

- **好きです。付き合ってください。** — 가장 격식
- **好きです。** — 강한 호감
- **俺の彼女になってください** — 남성 → 여성, 캐주얼
- **俺のことが好きか?** — 매우 캐주얼 (드라마틱)

## Cultural Notes

**이 표현의 문화적 무게**:
서양의 "Will you be my girlfriend?"와 비슷하지만 **더 격식적**.
일본에서는 告白 자체가 큰 이벤트로 인식.

**위치 선택**도 중요**: 공원, 카페, 해변, 옥상 등 분위기 있는 곳.
밤이 더 로맨틱.

**응답**:
- 긍정: 「私も好きです」 (나도 좋아해)
- 보류: 「考えさせて」 (생각하게 해줘)
- 거절: 「ごめんなさい」 (미안)

## Related Expressions

- [[手繋いでもいい]] — 친밀 진전
- [[俺の彼女になってよ]] — 캐주얼

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-jp]] — 고백

japanese-dating-culture.md.title: Japanese Dating Culture
japanese-dating-culture.md.body:
# Japanese Dating Culture

**Type:** 文化ノート, 恋愛
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

일본 데이팅 문화는 **间接(間接)·儀礼(儀礼)·段階(段階)** 중심. 감정 표현은 段階적으로 진행되며, 敬語(경어)에서 タメ口(반말)로의 전환이 관계 진전의 핵심 신호. 告白(고백)은 **공식 이벤트**로, 명확한 의례.

## Setting

A first date in Japan: **カフェ, 動物園, 水族館, 映画, 散歩(산책)**. Often daytime (昼) for first date; evening for established couples. Quiet, conversational activities preferred over loud bars.

## Roles

- **告白する人 (kokuhaku suru hito)** — Confessor (traditionally male, but increasingly either)
- **告白される人 (kokuhaku sareru hito)** — Confessee
- **彼氏 / 彼女 (kareshita / kanojo)** — Boyfriend / girlfriend (after confession)
- **気になる人 (ki ni naru hito)** — "Person of interest" (pre-confession)

## Scenario: Typical First Date → Confession

1. **友達 (Tomodachi)** — Friends, met through school/work/hobby
2. **気になる / 片思い (Ki ni naru / Kataomoi)** — One or both feel attraction
3. **何回かデート (Nankai ka deeto)** — Several dates, group + 1-on-1
4. **告白 (Kokuhaku)** — Confession, often in person, often a "dramatic" moment
5. **付き合う (Tsukiau)** — Officially dating
6. **恋人 (Koibito)** — Established couple

## Cultural Insight

### 告白 (Kokuhaku) 文化

일본에서 告白 = 공식 이벤트. 서양의 "DTR" (Define The Relationship)보다 더 의례적이고 무게감 있음.

**典型的な流れ**:
1. 友達 (친구)
2. 気になる / 片思い (썸/짝사랑)
3. 何回かデート (몇 번 데이트)
4. 告白 (고백, in-person)
5. 付き合う (사귀다)
6. 恋人 (연인)

### 敬語 → タメ口 シフト

**가장 중요한 관계성 전환점**. 연인 사이의 가장 큰 신호 중 하나.

- 그 전: 「○○さん、好きです」
- 후: 「○○ちゃん、好き」

이 시점이 자연스럽게 정해지지 않으면 관계 진행이 막힐 수 있음.

### 脈あり vs 脈なし シグナル

**脈あり (myaku-ari) — 관심이 있는 신호**:
- 자주 연락 (먼저)
- 2人だけの時間 を求める
- ボディタッチ OK (어깨, 팔)
- 食事에 おごろうとする (Z세대: 割り勘 OK)
- 目を見て話す

**脈なし (myaku-nashi) — 관심 없는 신호**:
- 既読スルー (kidoku suru) — LINE 읽고 답장 안 함
- 短返信 (짧은 답장)
- グループに ばかり混ぜようとする
- 視線合わない

### 割り勘 vs おごり

**世代 차이**:
- 30代 이상: 男性 おごり 일반적
- Z世代: 割り勘 (각자 내기) 主流

**판단 기준**: 연인끼리 처음엔 おごり 가능. Z세대는 最初から 割り勘 가능.

### 個人空間 と 距離感

- 初対面: 1m 이상
- 友達: 50cm-1m
- 恋人: 30-50cm (터치 가능)
- 身体接触: 一般的に 서양보다 적음

### 季節と文化

- **クリスマス**: 恋人 일(day). 커플 행위, イルミネーション.
- **バレンタイン (2/14)**: 여성이 남성에게 チョコ. ホワイトデー (3/14): 남성이 답례.
- **お花見 (4월)**: 벚꽃 구경 데이트.
- **夏祭り (8월)**: 전통 축제 데이트.

### 既婚 / 同棲

- 同棲: 점차 보편화 (20-30대)
- 婚前 동거: 一般的

## Useful Phrases

- [[好き]] — I like you
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[可愛い]] — cute
- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[面白い]] — interesting/funny
- [[デート]] — date
- [[告白]] — confession
- [[付き合う]] — to date
- [[恋人]] — lover
- 好きです (suki desu) — I like you
- ありがとう (arigatou) — thank you
- おはよう / おやすみ — good morning / good night (intimacy signal)

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **ボディタッチ 너무 일찍** — 부담
- ❌ **LINE 너무 자주** — 부담
- ❌ **告白 너무 일찍** — 부담
- ❌ **敬語 → タメ口 너무 늦게** — 관계 막힘
- ❌ **既読スルー 続ける** — 끝
- ❌ **人前で大声で愛情表現** — 매우 부적절
- ❌ **食事のマナー 무시** (음료, 이쑤시개 등) — 일본에서 매우 부정적

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 目を見て話す (eye contact while talking) | 脈あり (interested) |
| 髪を触る (touching hair) | 脈あり (flirting) |
| ボディタッチ (light touch on arm) | 脈あり |
| 足を組む (crossing legs toward you) | 脈あり |
| 腕を組む (crossed arms) | 防御的 / 緊張 |
| 視線を逸らす (looking away) | 緊張 / 脈なし |
| ため口 (casual speech) | 親密 (intimate) — 関係進行中 |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[english-dating-culture]], [[spanish-dating-culture]], [[korean-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[好き]], [[告白]], [[付き合う]], [[恋人]]

## 一般的な失敗

- **ボディタッチ 너무 일찍** — 부담
- **LINE 너무 자주** — 부담
- **告白 너무 일찍** — 부담
- **敬語 → タメ口 너무 늦게** — 관계 막힘
- **既読スルー 続ける** — 끝

japanese-holidays-culture.md.body:
# 日本の祝日·祭日文化 (Japanese Holidays & Celebrations Culture)

**Type:** 祝日, 年中行事, 季節行事
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の祝日は**日历通りです**. クリスマスは恋人たちのイベント, 正月は家族聚集. 欧米とは大きく異なる.

## Overview (English)

Japanese holidays follow the **calendar precisely**. Christmas is a couple's event; New Year's is a family reunion. This is very different from Western norms.

## 主要な祝日

### 正月 (しょうがつ) — New Year

**最重要的年中行事**. 12月31日の「大晦日」(おおみそか) に：

- **年越し蕎麦** — 細く長く人生を
- **初詣** (はつもうで) — 新年の神社·寺院参抭
- **お年玉** (おとしだま) — ワイト封筒で子了に money

> 「明けましておめでとうございます！」
> "Happy New Year!"

### クリスマス — Christmas

日本的 Christmas:

- **恋人のイベント** — 男性が女性に礼物を贈る
- **肯德基 (KFC) の予約** — 日本のクリスマス鸡肉の伝統
- **ケーキ** — 苺ショートケーキ

> 「メリークリスマス！」
> "Merry Christmas!"

### 'autres 主要祝日

| 日付 | 祝日 | 備考 |
|------|------|------|
| 1月1日 | 元日 | 年始寒喧 |
| 2月14日 | バレンタインデー | 女性から男性に chocolateを贈呈 |
| 3月14日 | ホワイトデー | 男性が女性に礼物を贈る |
| 7月 | 七夕 | 星に願いを |
| 8月 | お盆 | 家族聚集·先祖的石 |
| 10月 | ハロウィン | 子供たちがコラー么.collecting |
| 11月23日 | 勤労感謝の日 | |

## Sources

- [[holidays-and-celebrations-jp]] — 祝日語彙

japanese-daily-life-culture.md.title: 日本の日常文化 (Japanese Daily Life Culture)
japanese-daily-life-culture.md.body:
# 日本の日常文化 (Japanese Daily Life Culture)

**Type:** 日常, 生活, 社会
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の日常生活は**規律·循環·調和**で特徴づけられる. 朝型生活·(Object)ゴミ分別·湯沸かし習慣·コンビニ依存.

## Overview (English)

Japanese daily life is characterized by **discipline, routine, and harmony**. Morning routines, garbage separation, the kotatsu/ofuro culture, and convenience store dependency define the rhythm.

## 1日の流れ

| 時間 | 活動 | Notes |
|------|------|-------|
| 6:00-7:00 | 起床·朝食 | 日本人は朝ごはんの文化 |
| 8:00-9:00 | 通勤 | 満員電車が名物 |
| 12:00-13:00 | 昼食 | 社は外食·コンビニ |
| 18:00-20:00 | 退社·夕食 | 飲み会 (nomikai) 文化 |
| 21:00-23:00 | 自由時間 | 漫画·ゲーム·youtube |
| 23:00-24:00 | 就寝 | 睡眠時間世界最短レベル |

## 重要な日常生活ルール

### ゴミ分別

日本のゴミ分別は**细致的**:

- **燃えるゴミ** (週2回)
- **資源ゴミ** (週1回)
- **ペットボトル** (週1回)
- **粗大ゴミ** (有料·事前連絡)

> 違反すると**指導**される。違反金あり。

### コンビニ文化

日本のコンビニは**何でも揃う**:

- ATM (伊予銀行·郵貯)
- チケット販売
- 宅配便 (ヤマト·佐川)
- コピー· FAX
- 税金·公共料金支払い
- 野菜·食品·医薬品

### 挨拶の連鎖

日本の日常挨拶:

| 時間帯 | 挨拶 | 返事 |
|--------|------|------|
| 朝 | おはようございます | おはよう |
| 昼 | こんにちは | こんにちは |
| 夜 | こんばんは | こんばんは |
| 别れ | さようなら/行ってきます | いってらっしゃい |

## Sources

- [[daily-life-basics-jp]] — 日常語彙

japanese-shopping-culture.md.title: 日本の買い物·お金の文化 (Japanese Shopping & Money Culture)
japanese-shopping-culture.md.body:
# 日本の買い物·お金の文化 (Japanese Shopping & Money Culture)

**Type:** 買い物, お金, 消費文化
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の買い物文化は**現金&電子決済主流**. コンビニ·百货店·ディスカウントストア并存. **税金が别表示**なことが最も面倒.

## Overview (English)

Japanese shopping culture is **cash and e-payment dominant**. Convenience stores, department stores, and discount stores coexist. **Tax shown separately** at checkout is the most confusing aspect.

## 價格表示

| 表示 | 意味 |
|------|------|
| **本体価格** | 税抜き価格 |
| **税込価格** | 税込み価格 |
| **税抜** | 税金别 |

> "これは税込みですか？"
> "Is this price with tax?"

## 店の種類

| 店 | Description | 価格帯 |
|----|-------------|--------|

japanese-food-culture.md.title: 日本食文化 (Japanese Food Culture)
japanese-food-culture.md.body:
# 日本食文化 (Japanese Food Culture)

**Type:** 食事, エチケット, 饮食儀礼
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

日本の食事文化は**「いただきます」**と**「ごちそうさま」**から構成. 食事は単なる栄養摂取ではなく、**人間関係の構築**手段.

## Overview (English)

Japanese food culture centers on **gratitude before and after meals**. Eating is not just nutrition — it's a tool for **building relationships**.

## 食事の基本原則

### 箸 (おはし) のエチケット

箸は最も重要な用餐具. 以下は禁忌:

- **箸を器に刺す** — 線香を彷彿とさせる
- **箸で食べ物を渡す** — 火葬の行為を彷彿とさせる
- **箸をなめる** — 幼稚 perception
- **立っている箸で器を指す** — 強調の程度が強すぎる

### 外食文化

日本の外食代は**手頃な価格**で質の高い食物:

- **定食 (ていしょく)** — ご飯+汁+主菜+副菜 = 一式
- **コンビニ食** — コンビ二食品は驚くほど高品质
- **中華·イタリアン·和食·洋食** — 選択肢が豊富

> 「おすすめは何ですか？」
> "What do you recommend?"

## 食事の進行

1. **入店** — 店に入る前にメニューを見る
2. **注文** — 「すみません/order お願いします」
3. **用餐** — 「いただきます」（食事を始める合図）
4. **退出** — 「ごちそうさま」（食事を終える合図）
5. **会計** — 「お会計お願いします」

## Sources

- [[food-and-dining-jp]] — 食事語彙

## Usage

## Cultural Notes

- **Enamorado**: 감정 상태 ("I'm in love with you")
- **Te quiero**: 가벼운 사랑 ("I love you")

**응답**:
- 긍정: "Yo también estoy enamorado/a de ti"

subirse-al-higado.md.title: subirse al hígado
subirse-al-higado.md.body:
# subirse al hígado

**Literal Translation:** ím ao figado (subir al hígado)

**Meaning:** Enojarse mucho, irritarse profundamente. La cólera sube desde el estómago hasta la cabeza pero se siente como si se aposentara en el hígado.

**Usage Context:** Coloquial, en España y buena parte de Latinoamérica. Expresión de disgusto profundo, no de furia explosiva.

## Examples

- *A los hombres se les **subieron al hígado** las suspicacias y empezaron a rezongar.*
- *Se me **subió al hígado** que no me agradecieran.*
- *Esas críticas se le **subieron al hígado**.*

## Cultural Background

Metáfora del cuerpo como contenedor de emociones. El hígado, junto con el corazón y el pecho, es sede clásica de la ira y el rencor en la cultura hispana ("me da en el hígado", "arderle el hígado"). García Márquez elige "subirse" (acción ascendente, contenido acumulado) frente a "arder" (acción presente) para sugerir enojo que se va gestando.

## Similar Expressions

- [[arder-el-higado]] - forma más común
- [[no-poder-tragar]] - enojo contenido
- [[estar-hasta-las-ancas]] - estar harto (Argentina)

## Sources

- [[el-ahogado-mas-hermoso-del-mundo]]

spanish-holidays-culture.md.title: Spanish Holidays & Celebrations Culture
spanish-holidays-culture.md.body:
# Spanish Holidays & Celebrations Culture

**Type:** Notas culturales, fiestas, tradiciones, seasonal events
**Region:** España / México / Argentina / Colombia / Chile / Latin America

## Overview

Spanish-speaking countries are among the **most celebration-oriented cultures** in the world. Spain alone has over 200 festivals; Latin America has vibrant regional celebrations tied to religion, independence, and agriculture. Holidays are **family-centered** and often involve **food**, **music**, and **dance**.

## Overview (Korean)

스페인어권은 세계에서 가장 축제 중심 문화 중 하나. 축제는 가족 중심이며 음식·음악·무도과 결합.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Spain: Major Festivals

| Festival | When | Where | Description |
|----------|-------|-------|-------------|
| **La Tomatina** | Aug 29 | Buñol | World's biggest tomato fight |
| **San Fermín** | Jul 7-14 | Pamplona | Running of the bulls |
| **Fallas de Valencia** | Mar 15-19 | Valencia | Giant puppet burning |
| **Semana Santa** | Easter week | Sevilla | Religious processions |

**Semana Santa** (Holy Week) in Spain:
- Religious parades through streets
- **Semana Santa food**: torrijas (bread pudding), pestiños (honey pastries)
- Big family meals, many businesses closed

### Mexico: Día de los Muertos

**November 1-2**: Day of the Dead
- Altars (*ofrendas*) with photos of deceased relatives
- Marigolds (*cempasúchil*) guide spirits
- Pan de muerto (sweet bread) + calaveras (sugar skulls)
- NOT sad — **celebratory** of life and death

> "Los muertos no están muertos mientras los vivimos en la memoria." (= The dead are not dead as long as we carry them in memory.)

### Latin America: Fiestas Patrias

Each country celebrates independence with major holidays:

| Country | Holiday | Date | What |
|---------|---------|------|------|
| Mexico | Independencia | Sep 16 | Grito de Dolores |
| Argentina | Independencia | Jul 9 | Parades, asado |
| Colombia | Independencia | Jul 20 | Parades |

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | Phrase | Context |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Greeting at party | "¡Buenas noches!" | After dark |
| Toast | "¡Salud!" | Spain (also means "bless you") |
| Toast (LatAm) | "¡Chin chin!" / "¡Salud!" | Cheers |
| Thanking host | "¡Gracias por todo!" | After meal |

## Sources

- [[fiestas-y-celebraciones]] — holiday vocabulary

spanish-travel-culture.md.title: Spanish Travel Culture
spanish-travel-culture.md.body:
# Spanish Travel Culture

**Type:** Notas culturales, travel, Spain / Latin America
**Region:** España / México / Argentina / Colombia / Chile / general Hispanohablantes

## Overview

Spanish-speaking countries (especially Spain) have a **relaxed, tapas-based** travel culture. Time is flexible ("Spain is 1 hour behind Germany"), meals are late (dinner at 21-22h), and **personal interaction** is valued over efficiency.

## Overview (Korean)

스페인어권 여행 문화는 **타파스 중심의 여유로운** 문화. 시간은 유연하고, 식사는 늦으며, 개인적交流가 효율보다 중요.

## Key Cultural Traits

### Spain: The "Hora Española"

Spain runs on a different clock:

| Event | Spain (local) | Northern Europe (local) |
|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| Breakfast | 7-9h | 6-8h |
| Lunch | 14-16h | 11-13h |
| Dinner | 21-22h | 18-20h |
| Night life | Midnight+ | 22-23h |

> "Spain operates on '西班牙時間' — everything is an hour later."

### Tapas Culture

**Tapas** = small dishes shared between people. The word origin: "tapa" = lid (historically, a slice of bread over a wine glass to keep flies out).

**Rules of tapas:**
- Go to several bars, have 1-2 tapas at each
- Share with your group
- Stand at the bar (not always sitting)
- "La cuenta" (the check) — often split informally

> "¿Qué tapas recomienda?" — What tapas do you recommend?

### Transportation

| Country | Norm |
|---------|------|
| Spain | Subtle queues; don't push |
| Mexico | Boarding buses can be informal |
| Argentina | "Colectivo" culture; muy flexible |

## Phrases & Register

| Situation | Spain | Latin America |
|-----------|-------|---------------|
| Asking for directions | "¿Dónde está...?" | same |
| Bus ticket | "¿Dónde puedo comprar el billete?" | "¿Dónde compro el pasaje?" |
| Train | Renfe (Spain) | Trenes vary by country |

## Sources

- [[first-travel-spain]] — Spain travel source
- [[viaje-aventura]] — adventure travel

food/cebolla.md.title: cebolla
food/cebolla.md.body:
# cebolla

**Part of Speech:** sustantivo femenino

**Definition:** Planta hortense de la familia de las liliáceas, con bulbo comestible, de olor y sabor fuertes. Provoca lágrimas al cortarla.

**Etymology:** Del latín *cepulla*, diminutivo de *cepa* (cabeza,tronco).

## Examples

- *Mientras las **cebollas** que se estaban cociendo en el horno despedían un vapor penetrante...*
- *Pela una **cebolla** y sofríela en aceite de oliva.*
- *Lloraba como una **cebolla** pelada.* (modismo mexicano coloquial)

## Related Terms

- [[ajo]] - similar, pariente de la cebolla
- [[puerro]] - variedad de cebolla
- [[chalota]] - cebolla suave

## Cultural Notes

En México, pelar cebollas en la cocina es metáfora de **llanto femenino silencioso** en el ámbito doméstico. En *Como agua para chocolate*, las lágrimas de Tita al pelar cebollas se mezclan con la comida y producen efectos mágicos. La frase "llorar como una cebolla" es coloquial y se aplica a llanto intenso.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]

business/matriarca.md.title: matriarca
business/matriarca.md.body:
# matriarca

**Part of Speech:** sustantivo femenino

**Definition:** Mujer que ejerce el poder o la autoridad principal en una familia o grupo. Madre que gobierna con mano firme.

**Etymology:** Del latín *mater* (madre) + griego *archē* (mando, gobierno).

## Examples

- *Su madre, doña Carmen, era la **matriarca** de la familia De la Garza.*
- *La **matriarca** decidió que el menor no se casaría.*
- *Es una **matriarca** respetada por todos sus hijos y nietos.*

## Related Terms

- [[patriarca]] - versión masculina
- [[cabeza-de-familia]] - neutro
- [[matriarcado]] - sistema social

## Cultural Notes

En Latinoamérica rural del siglo XX, la **matriarca** tenía poder absoluto sobre la vida de las hijas solteras, especialmente en lo referente al matrimonio. **Mama Elena** en *Como agua para chocolate* es el arquetipo: su autoridad es ley indiscutible, respaldada por la "tradición sagrada". El conflicto novela-matriarca es uno de los grandes temas de la literatura latinoamericana.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[mexico-patriarcado-tradicion]]

business/heredar.md.title: heredar
business/heredar.md.body:
# heredar

**Part of Speech:** verbo transitivo

**Definition:** Recibir bienes, derechos o títulos de una persona que ha muerto. Por extensión, recibir algo de un antecesor.

**Etymology:** Del latín *hereditare*, de *heres, heredis* (heredero).

## Examples

- *Pedro acababa de **heredar** una fortuna de su padre.*
- *Heredó los ojos azules de su abuela.*
- *Heredó una tradición familiar de cocina.*

## Related Terms

- [[herencia]] - sustantivo
- [[heredero]] - quien hereda
- [[legado]] - lo que se deja

## Cultural Notes

En el México revolucionario (época de la novela), las **herencias** determinan el estatus social y las posibilidades matrimoniales. Pedro puede casarse con quien quiera precisamente porque acaba de **heredar una fortuna**. En la novela, además, se "heredan" cargas: Tita hereda de Mama Elena el mandato de cuidar a la madre.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]

nature/sollozar.md.title: sollozar
nature/sollozar.md.body:
# sollozar

**Part of Speech:** verbo intransitivo

**Definition:** Llorar con inspiraciones y espiraciones entrecortadas, casi sin poder hablar. Llanto con hipo.

**Etymology:** Del latín *singultire* (sollozar, hipar), emparentado con *sollozo*.

## Examples

- *Tita **sollozaba** en la cocina mientras pelaba las cebollas.*
- *El niño **sollozaba** después de la pesadilla.*
- *No pudo evitar **sollozar** al recibir la noticia.*

## Related Terms

- [[llorar]] - general
- [[gemir]] - quejarse con sonido
- [[hipar]] - sollozar con hipo marcado
- [[lagrimear]] - llorar levemente

## Cultural Notes

**Sollozar** es llanto profundo, no simple lagrimeo. En la literatura latinoamericana, los personajes que **sollozan en la cocina** están elaborando el duelo en privado. La imagen de Tita sollozando mientras cocina es la primera pintura emocional de la novela.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]

business/anhelar.md.title: anhelar
business/anhelar.md.body:
# anhelar

**Part of Speech:** verbo transitivo

**Definition:** Desear algo con vehemencia y persistencia. Ansiary con intensidad.

**Etymology:** Del latín *anhelare* (respirar con dificultad), de *anhelitus* (aliento). El deseo intenso se asoció metafóricamente al jadeo.

## Examples

- *Tita **anhelaba** casarse con Pedro.*
- *Anhelaba volver a ver a su hermana después de años.*
- ***Anhelamos** un verano tranquilo.*

## Related Terms

- [[desear]] - más general
- [[ansiar]] - deseo intenso (sinónimo)
- [[suspirar-por]] - deseo con lamento
- [[esperar]] - deseo con expectativa

## Cultural Notes

**Anhelar** tiene un matiz más profundo y casi poético que *desear*. Se usa más en literatura y habla elevada. En el contexto de Tita, **anhelar** a Pedro no es solo quererlo, es estar toda la vida orientada hacia él. La palabra tiene peso filosófico: del latín *anhelare* (jadear), como si el deseo mismo fuera un esfuerzo físico del cuerpo.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]

business/desafiar.md.title: desafiar
business/desafiar.md.body:
# desafiar

**Part of Speech:** verbo transitivo

**Definition:** Afrontar con decisión algo o a alguien que se considera un obstáculo. Oponerse, retar.

**Etymology:** Del francés antiguo *desafier* (romper la fe), de *des-* (privativo) + *afier* (confiar).

## Examples

- *Tita no se atrevía a **desafiar** la autoridad de su madre.*
- *Pedro **desafió** la tradición al casarse con Rosaura para estar cerca de Tita.*
- *Los jóvenes **desafiaron** las reglas del pueblo.*

## Related Terms

- [[retar]] - forma más coloquial
- [[afrontar]] - encarar
- [[enfrentarse-a]] - oponerse directamente
- [[obedecer]] - antónimo

## Cultural Notes

**Desafiar** en el contexto de la novela es un verbo cargado: Mama Elena no se **desafía** porque su autoridad es "ley sagrada". Cualquier **desafío** se castiga con la exclusión. La estructura familiar patriarcal-matriarcal solo permite **desafío** indirecto: Tita cocina, Tita transforma, Tita se rebela a través de la comida.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[mexico-patriarcado-tradicion]]

food/someterse.md.title: someterse
food/someterse.md.body:
# someterse

**Part of Speech:** verbo pronominal

**Definition:** Sujetarse a la autoridad, fuerza o decisión de otro. Rendirse, obedecer.

**Etymology:** Del latín *submittĕre* (poner debajo), de *sub-* (debajo) + *mĭttĕre* (enviar, lanzar).

## Examples

- *Tita tuvo que **someterse** a la decisión de su madre.*
- *Los pueblos indígenas se **sometieron** al dominio colonial.*
- *No estoy dispuesto a **someterme** a esas reglas.*

## Related Terms

- [[obedecer]] - forma simple
- [[rendirse]] - cesar en la resistencia
- [[subyugarse]] - con matiz de opresión
- [[rebelarse]] - antónimo

## Cultural Notes

**Someterse** implica no solo obedecer, sino aceptar una posición de inferioridad. En la novela, Tita no se **somete** por convicción sino por estructura social: no tiene adónde ir, no tiene medios, no tiene otra mujer que la defienda. El **someterse** femenino en la novela es social, no psicológico.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[mexico-patriarcado-tradicion]]

food/molcajete.md.title: molcajete
food/molcajete.md.body:
# molcajete

**Part of Speech:** sustantivo masculino

**Definition:** Mortero de piedra volcánica (basalto) usado en la cocina mexicana para moler chiles, especias, salsas. Tiene forma de cuenco con una mano de piedra (el *tejolote*).

**Etymology:** Del náhuatl *molcaxitl*, compuesto por *molli* (salsa, guiso) + *caxitl* (cajete, escudilla).

## Examples

- *Muele los chiles en el **molcajete** con un poco de sal.*
- *La salsa del **molcajete** tiene otra textura que la de licuadora.*
- *Mi abuela usaba el **molcajete** todos los días.*

## Related Terms

- [[tejolote]] - el mazo del molcajete
- [[metate]] - piedra para moler maíz
- [[salsas]] - preparados con molcajete

## Cultural Notes

El **molcajete** es un utensilio prehispánico (azteca) que sigue vivo en la cocina mexicana contemporánea. Aparece con frecuencia en la literatura y cine mexicanos como marcador de autenticidad. Tita, cuya cocina es refugio y tradición, sería usuaria natural del **molcajete**. La palabra proviene del náhuatl, lo que muestra el sustrato indígena vivo del español mexicano.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]

recetario-como-estructura.md.title: estructura de recetario (Esquivel)
recetario-como-estructura.md.body:
# estructura de recetario (Esquivel)

**Overview:** *Como agua para chocolate* es la primera novela de estructura **recetario** en la literatura hispanoamericana. Cada capítulo se abre con una receta, narrada en segunda persona (tú), que se entrelaza con la ficción. La novela es, literalmente, un libro de cocina + novela a la vez.

## Key Points

- **12 capítulos = 12 meses = 12 recetas**: enero a diciembre
- **Las recetas son segunda persona**: "Pela la cebolla, llora con ella..."
- **Las recetas son narrativas, no solo instructivas**: cuentan el estado emocional
- **Los ingredientes funcionan como símbolos**: cebolla = llanto, masa = tradición, chiles = pasión
- **El lector se convierte en cocinero**: puede seguir las recetas mientras lee
- **Decisión formal radical**: equiparar literatura con cocina

## Language Connections

- [[receta]] - la unidad básica
- [[cebolla]] - ingrediente simbólico
- [[masa]] - base de la cocina mexicana
- [[a-fuego-lento]] - técnica y tiempo
- [[ingredientes]] - lo que se mezcla

## Influencia

- Novela de Esquivel (1989) → éxito internacional
- **Frontera** de la escritora **Gloria Anzaldúa** (1987): teoría de frontera (mezcla de géneros) es coetánea
- **Chick lit** posterior y *food writing* deben algo a Esquivel
- La novela anticipa el interés cultural por la **cocina como patrimonio inmaterial** (UNESCO, s. XXI)

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[receta]]
- [[cocina-espacio-femenino]]

realismo-magico-marquez.md.title: realismo mágico (García Márquez)
realismo-magico-marquez.md.body:
# realismo mágico (García Márquez)

**Overview:** Corriente literaria latinoamericana en la que lo sobrenatural, lo mítico o lo imposible se presenta con la misma naturalidad que los hechos cotidianos. Los personajes no se sorprenden ante lo extraordinario.

## Key Points

- Lo prodigioso se describe con tono de crónica, sin explicaciones racionales
- Tiempo cíclico, no lineal: el pasado y el presente se entrelazan
- Realidad e imaginación son interdependientes
- Subversión sutil de la realidad sin fracturarla

## Language Connections

- [[promontorio]] - el escenario ambiguo al inicio del cuento
- [[altivez]] - tratamiento reverencial de lo extraordinario
- [[bobo]] - la cualidad paradójica del personaje mítico
- [[desvalido]] - la transformación por el duelo colectivo

## Fuentes principales

- Gabriel García Márquez - *Cien años de soledad* (1967), obra cumbre
- *El ahogado más hermoso del mundo* (1968) - ejemplo compacto
- Isabel Allende, Juan Rulfo, Alejo Carpentier - otros autores mayores

## Sources

- [[el-ahogado-mas-hermoso-del-mundo]]
- [[pueblo-costero-funeral]]
